title: Armed forces occupations
rationale: Military work increasingly uses digital systems for planning, intelligence, and logistics, so AI can improve coordination and analysis. But the occupation still depends heavily on physical presence, field execution, and high-stakes human judgment.

title: Managers
rationale: Managers spend a large share of time on planning, reporting, communication, and decision support, all of which are being accelerated by AI. The role still needs accountability, relationship management, and organizational judgment, so AI is more reshaping than fully replacing it.

title: Professionals
rationale: Professional work is often document-heavy, analytical, and digital, making it highly exposed to AI tools that draft, summarize, search, and reason over information. Many professional roles still retain human responsibility and domain expertise, but throughput is likely to change materially.

title: Technicians and associate professionals
rationale: These roles combine technical systems, monitoring, records, and applied problem solving, which gives AI many entry points to assist or automate portions of the work. Exposure is meaningful, but the work usually still includes equipment, field context, or human-facing execution.

title: Clerical support workers
rationale: Clerical support work is centered on documents, forms, scheduling, records, and routine communication, which are among the most AI-amenable workflows. The occupation is highly exposed because much of its output is structured digital information.

title: Service and sales workers
rationale: AI can help with recommendations, scripts, scheduling, and customer operations, but the core of service and sales work still relies heavily on physical presence, persuasion, and real-time interaction. That keeps exposure moderate rather than extreme.

title: Skilled agricultural, forestry and fishery workers
rationale: These jobs are rooted in physical environments, biological variability, machinery, and outdoor conditions that current AI alone cannot fully handle. AI may improve planning and monitoring, but it does not directly replace much of the day-to-day work.

title: Craft and related trades workers
rationale: Trades work remains hands-on and site-specific, with a strong need for dexterity, adaptation, and physical execution. AI can support estimation, documentation, and troubleshooting, but the core occupation remains relatively resilient.

title: Plant and machine operators, and assemblers
rationale: These roles can be affected by AI through process optimization, predictive maintenance, and increasing automation around machinery and workflows. Exposure is meaningful, but it is constrained by physical equipment, safety, and industrial operating environments.

title: Elementary occupations
rationale: Elementary occupations include many routine tasks that are vulnerable to automation in principle, but the work often still happens in physical environments with messy real-world constraints. AI can change coordination and screening, though direct substitution remains uneven.
